macro_segments.title: The Contradiction Between Two Mishnayot Regarding Whether a City Counts Against the 2000 Amot Boundary

micro_segments.title: The apparent contradiction: one Mishnah says placing an eiruv outside a city means what you gain on one side you lose on the other, while another Mishnah says if your 2000 amot ends in the middle of a city you must stop there
Rav Yehoshua ben Levi's resolution: if the city is completely within the 2000 amot it doesn't count against you, but if the 2000 ends in the middle of a city it does count against you
Rav Idi describes this distinction as divrei neviyut (words of prophecy) because it wasn't explicitly taught in the Mishnah
The Gemara attempts to demonstrate this distinction from the Mishnayot and confirms that the city counting against you when you end in its middle is indeed clear from the text

macro_segments.title: The Distinction of Two Cases: Big City and Small City Traveling Between One Another

micro_segments.title: Rav Nachman discusses different versions of a Mishnah about whether people of a small city can go through a big city, presenting two possible girsaot (textual versions)
One version: people of big city can go through small city, small city cannot go through big city, explained as a scenario where you start measuring from your city
Another version: both can go through each other, explained as a scenario where people put their eiruv in the other city and become members of that city
The Gemara clarifies the Mishnah text is incomplete and must be reconstructed to contrast two cases: measuring without eiruv versus placing eiruv in the other city

macro_segments.title: Cities on Precarious Terrain: The Retaining Wall Requirement

micro_segments.title: Rav Yosef quoting Rav Huna: a city built on the edge of a river or wadi requires a four-amah barrier or retaining wall to be counted as a city, otherwise it is measured as individual houses

Abaye questions why the barrier must be four amot instead of four tefachim like other cases, and Rav Yosef explains that here the space is actually used, so a functional barrier is needed rather than a symbolic one
Rav Yosef brings a Baraita about two cities on a slope, Gadgader and Chamtan, where Rabbi Meir allowed people from the higher city to descend to the lower but not vice versa
The Gemara suggests Rav Yosef's explanation: Gadgader built a retaining wall while Chamtan did not, so only Gadgader counts as a stable city

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanations for the Slope Cities Dispute

micro_segments.title: Rav Dimi explains the distinction politically: Gadgader and Chamtan were at war, and Rabbi Meir issued a takana forbidding contact based on the danger of aggression when they are on home turf and intoxicated on Shabbat

The Gemara questions why the takana only applies to Shabbat and why the people of Chamtan would not attack visitors from Gadgader, resolving that Gadgader is more powerful and will only act aggressively on their own territory
Rav Safra proposes a different explanation: the city was shaped like a rainbow, creating a complex geometry that affects who can travel where based on whether they start inside or outside
Tosafot explains Rav Safra's view: people starting inside the rainbow city benefit from the squaring off rule and can reach further, while people starting outside cannot access the same distances
Rav Dimi bar Chinnena offers a simpler explanation: it was simply a case of a big city and small city where the big city was not fully included in the small city's 2000-amah boundary

macro_segments.title: The Nature of City Membership: Can an Eiruv in a Different City Make You Part of That City?

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah states that if you put your eiruv in a different city you can go the entire city and 2000 amot outside it, making you effectively a member of that city

Rabbi Akiva disputes this: he says placing your eiruv in a city does not make you a member; you only get 2000 amot from where your eiruv is placed, and to become a member you must actually reside in the city when Shabbat begins
The Rabbis respond to Rabbi Akiva by noting that even in a cave, if there are people living there, placing your eiruv makes you part of the cave's residents and entitled to the whole space
The Gemara notes that according to the Rabbis a cave with inhabitants is like a city, while according to Rabbi Akiva you must actually be physically present to benefit from city status

macro_segments.title: Desolate Cities and Ghost Towns: When Does a City Without Inhabitants Count as a City?

micro_segments.title: Rav Yehuda in the name of Shmuel: if someone actually begins Shabbat in a desolate city, they can traverse the entire city and 2000 amot beyond, even though nobody lives there
However, if you only place your eiruv in a desolate city, you do not get the same benefit—you only get 2000 amot from where your eiruv is placed
Rabbi Eliezer disputes both limitations: whether you are physically present or just place your eiruv in a desolate city, you get the whole city and 2000 amot

The Gemara questions whether Rabbi Eliezer's view applies only to desolate cities or to inhabited cities as well, ultimately attributing the debate to the Rabbis versus Rabbi Eliezer on the status of a desolate city
The Gemara brings a Baraita equating a desolate city to a cave, showing both are only fully accessible if you are personally present when Shabbat begins, not merely by placing your eiruv

macro_segments.title: The Case of Mar Yehudah and the Communal Placing of Eiruv in the Synagogue

micro_segments.title: Mar Yehudah found the people of Mafrachta placing their eiruv in the synagogue of Agogva and advised them to place it more inside to extend their permitted territory, being cautious of Rabbi Akiva's position
The Gemara clarifies that the synagogue in this case is treated as a city-like space because people actually live or gather there, making a placed eiruv sufficient for those who live in the courtyard
Rava responds that we do not follow Rabbi Akiva's stringent position on eruvin; rather, we follow the lenient view, making it unnecessary to place the eiruv in the deepest interior

macro_segments.title: Introduction to the Problem of Non-Jews and Those Who Reject the Eiruv in a Shared Courtyard

micro_segments.title: The Mishnah states that if you live with a non-Jew or someone who does not believe in eiruv in your courtyard, that person makes it forbidden for you to make an eiruv
Rabbi Eliezer ben Yaakov disputes this: he says the problem only arises if there are two Jews in the courtyard who refuse to join the eiruv, not if there is a non-Jew or a single dissident
A story about Rabban Gamliel and a Sadducee living in their alleyway: Gamliel instructed his household to quickly move their vessels out before the Sadducee could take his out and prohibit them
Rashi explains that a Sadducee, unlike a non-Jew, can waive his rights and allow the eiruv, but the Rabbis must assert their rights before he changes his mind and reclaims them
Rabbi Yehudah presents an alternative version where the Sadducee can always reclaim his rights, so the Rabbis must use the courtyard while he has not yet objected
